docs.title: Evidence Profile
docs.text:
• Overall confidence: medium-high to high for the source-specific claims listed below; lower for broad extrapolation beyond the reported system.
  • Evidence profile: mouse high-fat diet obesity plus allergic airway inflammation; lung ILC2/ILC3 enumeration and cytokine-associated disease readouts.
  • Batch 3 synthesis role: ILC2/ILC3 obesity-associated allergic airway disease.

docs.title: Why It Matters Here
docs.text: This source links metabolic disease context to lung allergic inflammation and places both ILC2s and ILC3s in obesity-exacerbated AHR rather than treating asthma as only a lean type 2 model.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• High-fat diet-induced obesity exacerbated allergic airway inflammatory features, including eosinophilia and AHR, in the reported mouse model.
  • The source text reports increased total and cytokine-expressing lung ILC2s and ILC3s in obese allergic airway disease compared with lean allergic controls.
  • The paper supports obesity as a context that can reshape innate lymphoid contributions to allergic airway disease.
  • This should be curated as mouse metabolic-asthma evidence, not as a direct human obesity-asthma causal claim.

docs.title: Claim-Level Confidence
docs.text:
• High confidence: this source supports a mouse-model link between HFD-induced obesity, worsened allergic airway disease, and altered lung ILC2/ILC3 responses.
  • Medium-high confidence: the source supports ILC2/ILC3 involvement in obesity-exacerbated allergic inflammation, but exact mediator hierarchy should be checked against figures before detailed reuse.
  • Low confidence: broad extrapolation to human obesity-associated asthma phenotypes is not justified without human cohort evidence.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• 17 NUMBER 7 JULY 2016 nature immunology p e r s p e c t i v e I n n at e ly m p h o I d c e l l s Innate lymphoid cells (ILCs) are the most recently discovered group of immune cells.
  • We discuss here the current knowledge on the appearance of ILC subsets during evolution and propose how the connection between ILCs and T cells contributes to the robustness of immunity and hence to the fitness of the hosts.
  • Natural killer (NK) cells were subsequently identified as non-T, non-B lymphocytes with the capacity for spontaneous or ‘natural’ cytotoxicity activity against tumor cells without the need for prior immunization 8,9.
  • These cells were called ‘innate lymphoid cells’ (ILCs), and this designation has been expanded to include the related subsets of ILC1 cells, ILC2 cells, ILC3 cells, NK cells and LTi cells 11 (Fig.
  • Since their identification, ILCs have been shown to contribute to defense against infection and to wound healing11–13.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Cysteinyl leukotriene E 4 activates human group 2 innate lymphoid cells and enhances the effect of prostaglandin D 2 and epithelial cytokines Maryam Salimi, DPhil, MD, a Linda St €oger, MSc, b,c Wei Liu, MSc, b Simei Go, BSc, b Ian Pavord, F Med Sci, b Paul Klenerman, F Med Sci, d Graham Ogg, DPhil, FRCP, a and Luzheng Xue, PhD b,c Oxford, United Kingdom Background: Group 2 innate lymphoid cells (ILC2s) are a potential innate source of type 2 cytokines in the pathogenesis of allergic conditions.
  • Epithelial cytokines (IL-33, IL-25, and thymic stromal lymphopoietin [TSLP]) and mast cell mediators (prostaglandin D 2 [PGD2]) are critical activators of ILC2s.
  • Objectives: We sought to determine the role of cysLTs and their relationship with other ILC2 stimulators in the activation of human ILC2s.
  • The effects of cysLTs, PGD 2, IL-33, IL-25, TSLP, and IL-2 alone or in combination on ILC2s were deﬁned by using chemotaxis, apoptosis, ELISA, Luminex, quantitative RT-PCR, and ﬂow cytometric assays.
  • Results: Human ILC2s expressed the LT receptor CysLT 1, levels of which were increased in atopic subjects.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• The emerging field of innate lymphoid cell (ILC) biol- ogy has progressed rapidly, with many studies suggest- ing important roles for these cells in immunity, tissue development and remodelling1–8.
  • ILCs have been cate- gorized into three main groups based on their similarity to T helper (T H) cell subsets9 (FIG.
  • In this Review, we do not focus on the biology of lymphoid tissue-inducer (LTi) cells and natural killer (NK) cells or the roles of ILCs in cancer10–14, pregnancy15 or transplantation 16.
  • Instead, we focus on the role of ILC1s, ILC2s and ILC3s in autoimmune and inflamma- tory diseases affecting various tissues.
  • In particular, we focus on human data and discuss potential therapeutic strategies for targeting ILCs in inflammatory disease.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• However, it is still unclear how ILCs are regulated in the duration of intestinal inﬂam- mation.
  • Here, we identify a regulatory subpopulation of ILCs (called ILCregs) that exists in the gut and harbors a unique gene identity that is distinct from that of ILCs or regulatory T cells (Tregs).
  • During in- ﬂammatory stimulation, ILCregs can be induced in the intestine and suppress the activation of ILC1s and ILC3s via secretion of IL-10, leading to protec- tion against innate intestinal inﬂammation.
  • Moreover, TGF-b1 is induced by ILCregs during the innate in- testinal inﬂammation, and autocrine TGF-b1 sustains the maintenance and expansion of ILCregs.
  • There- fore, ILCregs play an inhibitory role in the innate im- mune response, favoring the resolution of intestinal inﬂammation.

docs.title: Evidence Profile
docs.text:
• Overall confidence: medium-high to high for the source-specific claims listed below; lower for broad extrapolation beyond the reported system.
  • Evidence profile: single-cell/transcriptomic ILC2 states, NMUR1 expression, NMU stimulation, IL-25/IL-33 alarmin context, allergen challenge.

docs.title: Why It Matters Here
docs.text: This source is a foundational mouse neuroimmune ILC2 paper and pairs with the newer human NMU/NMUR1 asthma evidence.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• The source reports that Nmur1 is preferentially expressed by ILC2s at steady state and after IL-25 stimulation.
  • NMU activates ILC2s in vitro and co-administration with IL-25 amplifies allergic inflammation in vivo.
  • Loss of NMU-NMUR1 signaling reduces ILC2 frequency and effector function after allergen challenge.
  • This paper establishes neuroimmune amplification as a core ILC2 regulatory branch.

docs.title: Claim-Level Confidence
docs.text:
• High confidence: NMU-NMUR1 signaling amplifies ILC2-driven allergic lung inflammation in the reported mouse systems.
  • Medium-high confidence: together with human airway NMUR1 evidence, this supports a conserved neuroimmune axis, but compartment and phenotype boundaries remain important.
  • Low confidence: NMU should not be treated as universally activating for all ILC2 states without context.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Li et al. report that Ahr restricts intestinal ILC2 function in a cell-intrinsic manner, suggesting a central role for gut adaptation of Ahr expression in regulating the ILC2-ILC3 balance.
  • The intestine harbors all ILC subsets, but how these cells are balanced to achieve immune ho- meostasis and mount appropriate responses during infection remains elusive.
  • Here, we show that aryl hydrocarbon receptor (Ahr) expression in the gut reg- ulates ILC balance.
  • Among ILCs, Ahr is most highly expressed by gut ILC2s and controls chromatin accessibility at the Ahr locus via positive feedback.
  • Ahr signaling suppresses Gﬁ1 transcription-factor- mediated expression of the interleukin-33 (IL-33) re- ceptor ST2 in ILC2s and expression of ILC2 effector molecules IL-5, IL-13, and amphiregulin in a cell- intrinsic manner.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• NEUROIMMUNOLOGY b2-adrenergic receptor–mediated negative regulation of group 2 innate lymphoid cell responses Saya Moriyama,1 Jonathan R.
  • Although recent studies identified group 2 innate lymphoid cells (ILC2s) as potent sources of type 2 cytokines, the molecular pathways controlling ILC2 responses are incompletely defined.
  • Here we demonstrate that murine ILC2s express the b 2-adrenergic receptor ( b2AR) and colocalize with adrenergic neurons in the intestine. b2AR deficiency resulted in exaggerated ILC2 responses and type 2 inflammation in intestinal and lung tissues.
  • Conversely,b 2AR agonist treatment was associated with impaired ILC2 responses and reduced inflammation in vivo.
  • Mechanistically, we demonstrate that theb2AR pathway is a cell-intrinsic negative regulator of ILC2 responses through inhibition of cell proliferation and effector function.

  • Publication year: 2018
  • DOI: 10.1038/mi.2017.41
  • Metadata source: nature (confidence: high)

docs.title: Ingest Mode
docs.text:
• Mode: focused manual crystallization mode
  • Meaning: this source page was read beyond bulk registration and now records source-specific biological claims, model context, assay directness, and reuse boundaries.
  • Durable synthesis status: selected high-confidence claims from this source were propagated into ILC2 entity/topic/digest pages with source links.

docs.title: Source Type
docs.text:
• primary mouse influenza host-defense and ILC2-function study
  • Evidence profile: H1N1 A/CA/04/2009 influenza challenge, IFN-gamma deficiency/neutralization, ILC2 deficiency, IL-5 dependence, viral load and tissue integrity readouts.
  • Knowledge note status: focused source note suitable for wiki synthesis, with caveats preserved below.

docs.title: Evidence Profile
docs.text:
• Overall confidence: high for source-specific claims explicitly tied to the reported model, species, assay, and outcome; lower for cross-disease or human translation unless directly tested.
  • Evidence tags: #cell/ILC2 #tissue/lung #topic/regulation #topic/pulmonary_disease #axis/ILC2_niche #axis/interferon_brake #axis/type2_immunity #status/focused_crystallization
  • Primary biological axis: This source adds a key counterintuitive viral-infection branch: IFN-gamma can increase influenza susceptibility by suppressing protective ILC2 output rather than by altering viral clearance.

docs.title: Why It Matters Here
docs.text: This source adds a key counterintuitive viral-infection branch: IFN-gamma can increase influenza susceptibility by suppressing protective ILC2 output rather than by altering viral clearance.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Influenza-induced IFN-gamma suppresses lung ILC2 function after H1N1 CA04 infection.
  • IFN-gamma deficiency increases ILC2 IL-5 and amphiregulin production and improves lung tissue integrity without changing ILC2 number, viral load, or viral clearance.
  • Anti-IFN-gamma treatment improves survival in an ILC2- and IL-5-dependent manner in the reported mouse system.
  • The paper supports separating ILC2 abundance from ILC2 functional output during viral infection.

docs.title: Claim-Level Confidence
docs.text:
• High confidence: in this mouse influenza model, IFN-gamma suppresses protective ILC2 IL-5/AREG-associated function.
  • Medium-high confidence: IFN-gamma neutralization improves survival in a source-supported preclinical model, but this is not a clinical recommendation.
  • Low confidence: the claim should not be generalized to all influenza strains, time points, or human disease without matching evidence.

docs.title: Methods and Context
docs.text:
• Species/context: mouse H1N1 influenza infection.
  • Assay directness: cytokine neutralization/deficiency and cell-dependence experiments tied to survival and tissue readouts.
  • Best wiki use: viral ILC2 repair/protection, IFN-gamma brake, and cell-function versus cell-number distinction.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• IFN-gamma is broadly protective in many infections; this source supports a specific lung ILC2-suppression branch in one influenza model.
  • Do not interpret unchanged viral load as meaning IFN-gamma is irrelevant to antiviral immunity in other settings.

docs.title: Contradiction and Supersession
docs.text:
• Contradiction status: this source should be integrated as a context-specific branch, not as a universal statement about ILC2 biology.
  • Supersession status: this source refines earlier ILC2 models by adding spatial, interferon, neuroimmune, developmental, or type 2 framework detail; it does not supersede lung repair, allergic inflammation, or ILC2 plasticity branches.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Leading Edge Review Innate Lymphoid Cells: 10 Years On Eric Vivier,1,2,* David Artis,3 Marco Colonna,4 Andreas Diefenbach,5,6,7 James P.
  • ILCs are largely tissue-resident cells and are deeply integrated into the fabric of tissues.
  • The discovery and investigation of ILCs over the past decade has changed our perception of immune regulation and how the immune system contributes to the maintenance of tissue homeostasis.
  • We now know that cytokine-producing ILCs contribute to mul- tiple immune pathways by, for example, sustaining appropriate immune responses to commensals and pathogens at mucosal barriers, potentiating adaptive immunity, and regulating tissue inﬂam- mation.
  • Critically, the biology of ILCs also extends beyond classical immunology to metabolic homeostasis, tissue remodeling, and dialog with the nervous system.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Recent studies suggest that ILCs are more diverse than originally proposed; this might reﬂect truly distinct lineages or adaptation of ILCs to disparate tissue microenvironments, known as plasticity.
  • While the regulation, timing, and magnitude of ILC and primary T cell responses differ, tissue-resident memory T cells may render ILCs redun- dant during secondary responses.
  • The unique impact of ILCs in immunity is probably embodied in the exten- sive array of surface and intracellular receptors that endow these cells with the ability to distinguish between normal and pathogenic components, interact with other cells, and calibrate their cytokine secretion accord- ingly.
  • Here I review recent advances in elucidating the diversity of ILCs and discuss their unique and redun- dant functions.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Intestinal homeostasis and tolerance are mediated through a multitude of immune mechanisms that act to reinforce barrier integrity, maintain the segregation and balance of commensal microbes, and ensure tissue health and regener- ation.
  • Here, we discuss the role of group 3 innate lymphoid cells (ILC3) as key regulators of intestinal health and highlight how increasing evidence implicates dysregulation of this innate immune cell population in the onset or progression of a broad range of clinically relevant pathologies.
  • Finally, we discuss how the next generation of immunotherapeutics may be utilized to target ILC3 in disease and restore gastrointestinal tolerance and tissue health.

docs.title: Evidence Profile
docs.text:
• Overall confidence: medium-high to high for the source-specific claims listed below; lower for broad extrapolation beyond the reported system.
  • Evidence profile: murine and human ILC2 assays; butyrate administration; IL-33 and Alternaria ILC2-driven airway inflammation models.
  • Batch 3 synthesis role: butyrate/HDAC inhibition of ILC2 AHR.

docs.title: Why It Matters Here
docs.text: This source adds microbiota-derived metabolite regulation to the lung ILC2-AHR model.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Butyrate, but not acetate or propionate, inhibited IL-13 and IL-5 production by murine ILC2s in the source text.
  • Systemic and local butyrate administration ameliorated ILC2-driven AHR and airway inflammation in reported models.
  • Butyrate inhibited ILC2 proliferation and reduced cytokine production in human ILC2s.
  • The mechanism is framed through HDAC inhibitory activity.

docs.title: Claim-Level Confidence
docs.text:
• High confidence: butyrate suppresses ILC2 cytokine output and ILC2-dependent AHR in the reported systems.
  • Medium-high confidence: human ILC2 cytokine suppression supports translational relevance, but clinical asthma efficacy is not established here.
  • Low confidence: do not generalize all SCFAs as equivalent regulators of ILC2s.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• IMMUNOLOGY S1P-dependent interorgan trafficking of group 2 innate lymphoid cells supports host defense Yuefeng Huang,1† Kairui Mao,2 Xi Chen,1 Ming-an Sun,3 Takeshi Kawabe,1 Weizhe Li,2 Nicholas Usher,1,4 Jinfang Zhu,1 Joseph F.
  • Germain 1,2† Innate lymphoid cells (ILCs) are innate counterparts of adaptive T lymphocytes, contributing to host defense, tissue repair, metabolic homeostasis, and inflammatory diseases.
  • ILCs have been considered to be tissue-resident cells, but whether ILCs move between tissue sites during infection has been unclear.
  • We show here that interleukin-25– or helminth-induced inflammatory ILC2s are circulating cells that arise from resting ILC2s residing in intestinal lamina propria.
  • They migrate to diverse tissues based on sphingosine 1-phosphate (S1P)–mediated chemotaxis that promotes lymphatic entry, blood circulation, and accumulation in peripheral sites, including the lung, where they contribute to anti-helminth defense and tissue repair.

For current synthesis, it should be used primarily as evidence for the source-specific claim that the Tumor Necrosis Factor Superfamily Member RANKL Suppresses Effector Cytokine Production in Group 3 Innate Lymphoid Cells, then upgraded or narrowed after manual review of the full methods, figures, and model system.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Bando et al. demonstrate that the TNF superfamily member RANKL suppresses the abundance and effector functions of intestinal CCR6 + ILC3s and that RANKL- mediated suppression occurs through ILC3-ILC3 interactions.
  • Here we found that the tumor necrosis factor (TNF) superfamily member receptor activator of nuclear factor kB ligand (RANKL) suppressed ILC3 activity in the intestine.
  • Deletion of RANKL in ILC3s and T cells increased C-C motif chemokine receptor 6 (CCR6) + ILC3 abundance and enhanced production of interleukin-17A (IL-17A) and IL-22 in response to IL-23 and during infection with the enteric mu- rine pathogen Citrobacter rodentium .
  • Additionally, CCR6 + ILC3s produced higher amounts of the master transcriptional regulator RORgt at steady state in the absence of RANKL.
  • RANKL-mediated suppression was independent of T cells, and instead occurred via interactions between CCR6 + ILC3s that ex- pressed both RANKL and its receptor, RANK.

docs.title: Why It Matters Here
docs.text: This source matters to the ILC-in-lung wiki because it appears to address lung/airway, asthma/allergy with a focus on ILC2, T cells. For current synthesis, it should be used primarily as evidence for the source-specific claim that tissue-Restricted Adaptive Type 2 Immunity Is Orchestrated by Expression of the Costimulatory Molecule OX40L on Group 2 Innate Lymphoid Cells, then upgraded or narrowed after manual review of the full methods, figures, and model system.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Halim et al. demonstrate that the local expansion of Th2 and Treg cells in response to the alarmin IL-33 is dependent on the expression of the costimulatory molecule OX40L by type 2 innate lymphoid cells (ILC2s), revealing a central role for the IL-33-ILC2-OX40L pathway in the orchestration of type 2 immunity.
  • Here we found that alarmin- induced expression of the co-stimulatory molecule OX40L on group 2 innate lymphoid cells (ILC2s) pro- vided tissue-restricted T cell co-stimulation that was indispensable for Th2 and regulatory T (Treg) cell re- sponses in the lung and adipose tissue.
  • Interleukin (IL)-33 administration resulted in organ-speciﬁc surface expression of OX40L on ILC2s and the concomitant expansion of Th2 and Treg cells, which was abolished upon deletion of OX40L on ILC2s ( Il7ra Cre/+Tnfsf4ﬂ/ﬂ mice).
  • Moreover, Il7raCre/+ Tnfsf4ﬂ/ﬂ mice failed to mount effective Th2 and Treg cell responses and corresponding adaptive type 2 pulmonary inﬂammation arising from Nippos- trongylus brasiliensis infection or allergen exposure.
  • Thus, the increased expression of OX40L in response to IL-33 acts as a licensing signal in the orchestration of tissue-speciﬁc adaptive type 2 immunity, without which this response fails to establish.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Sonnenberg1,2,3* Group 3 innate lymphoid cells (ILC3s) critically orchestrate host-microbe interactions in the healthy mammalian intestine and become substantially impaired in the context of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD).
  • However, the molecular pathways controlling the homeostasis of ILC3s remain incompletely defined.
  • Here, we identify that intestinal ILC3s are highly enriched in expression of genes involved in the circadian clock and exhibit diurnal oscillations of these pathways in response to light cues.
  • Classical ILC3 effector functions also exhibited diurnal oscillations, and lineage-specific deletion of BMAL1, a master regulator of the circadian clock, resulted in markedly reduced ILC3s selectively in the intestine.
  • BMAL1-deficient ILC3s exhibit impaired expression of Nr1d1 and Per3, hyperactivation of RORt-dependent target genes, and elevated proapoptotic pathways.

  • Metadata source: pubmed (confidence: high)
  • Original local title: Adventitial Stromal Cells Define Group 2 Innate Lymphoid Cell Tissue Niches

docs.title: Ingest Mode
docs.text:
• Mode: focused manual crystallization mode
  • Meaning: this source page was read beyond bulk registration and now records source-specific biological claims, model context, assay directness, and reuse boundaries.
  • Durable synthesis status: selected high-confidence claims from this source were propagated into ILC2 entity/topic/digest pages with source links.

docs.title: Source Type
docs.text:
• primary mouse tissue-imaging and stromal-niche study
  • Evidence profile: quantitative 3D imaging, flow cytometry, scRNA-seq, stromal-cell functional assays, helminth/type 2 perturbation, and ASC depletion in mouse lung and other tissues.
  • Knowledge note status: focused source note suitable for wiki synthesis, with caveats preserved below.

docs.title: Evidence Profile
docs.text:
• Overall confidence: high for source-specific claims explicitly tied to the reported model, species, assay, and outcome; lower for cross-disease or human translation unless directly tested.
  • Evidence tags: #cell/ILC2 #tissue/lung #topic/regulation #topic/pulmonary_disease #axis/ILC2_niche #axis/interferon_brake #axis/type2_immunity #status/focused_crystallization
  • Primary biological axis: This paper gives the wiki a spatial organizing principle for lung ILC2 biology: ILC2s are not only cytokine-responsive cells, but tissue-positioned lymphocytes embedded in adventitial stromal niches.

docs.title: Why It Matters Here
docs.text: This paper gives the wiki a spatial organizing principle for lung ILC2 biology: ILC2s are not only cytokine-responsive cells, but tissue-positioned lymphocytes embedded in adventitial stromal niches.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• ILC2s localize in a conserved adventitial niche around lung bronchi and larger vessels rather than being randomly distributed through lung tissue.
  • ILC2s are closely associated with fibroblast-like adventitial stromal cells (ASCs) that express IL-33 and TSLP.
  • ASC-derived TSLP supports ILC2 accumulation and activation in vitro, and ASC-derived IL-33 contributes to type 2 lymphocyte accumulation and function during helminth-driven lung inflammation.
  • ILC2s and IL-13 reciprocally promote ASC expansion and IL-33 expression, supporting a stromal-immune feedback loop.

docs.title: Claim-Level Confidence
docs.text:
• High confidence: mouse lung ILC2s occupy adventitial/peribronchovascular niches and are anatomically associated with IL-33/TSLP-expressing ASCs.
  • High confidence: the source supports a reciprocal ASC-ILC2-IL-13 circuit in type 2 inflammation.
  • Medium confidence: human or disease-general claims should be treated as conceptual extension unless matched human lung spatial evidence is available.

docs.title: Methods and Context
docs.text:
• Species/context: mouse lung and multiple tissues, with helminth/type 2 immune perturbation.
  • Assay directness: strong spatial and perturbation evidence for niche localization and stromal support.
  • Best wiki use: tissue niche, stromal IL-33/TSLP, reciprocal ILC2-stromal feedback, and interpretation of lung ILC2 position.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• Do not treat adventitial niche localization as proof that every disease-relevant ILC2 remains in that niche during inflammation.
  • Separate ASC-derived IL-33/TSLP support from epithelial alarmin release; both can regulate ILC2s but represent different tissue layers.

docs.title: Contradiction and Supersession
docs.text:
• Contradiction status: this source should be integrated as a context-specific branch, not as a universal statement about ILC2 biology.
  • Supersession status: this source refines earlier ILC2 models by adding spatial, interferon, neuroimmune, developmental, or type 2 framework detail; it does not supersede lung repair, allergic inflammation, or ILC2 plasticity branches.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Increased production of and sensitivity to PGD 2 is associated with poor asthma control.

  • After activation by PGD 2, ILC2s produce large amounts of type 2 cytokines, predominantly IL-13.

docs.title: Ingest Mode
docs.text:
• Mode: focused manual crystallization mode
  • Meaning: this source page has been manually upgraded for a priority crystallization pass and may support durable topic/entity/project/digest synthesis.

docs.title: Source Type
docs.text:
• primary chronic allergic asthma model study
  • SCF/c-Kit regulation of ILC2 activation source
  • stromal/growth-factor regulation source for chronic type 2 airway disease
  • crystallization status: manually promoted into batch 2 high-priority lung ILC source note

docs.title: Evidence Profile
docs.text:
• Overall confidence: high for SCF/c-Kit contributing to ILC2 activation and chronic allergic disease severity in the reported mouse systems.
  • Directness to this project: high for chronic allergic asthma ILC2 regulation; medium for human disease translation.
  • Evidence type: chronic allergic disease mouse model, SCF248 antibody, SCF conditional deletion and c-Kit-defective contexts, in vitro ILC2 activation.
  • Main biological axis: SCF/SCF248 -> c-Kit+ ILC2 activation -> type 2 cytokines, ILC2/eosinophil/mast cell accumulation, chronic asthma severity.

docs.title: Why It Matters Here
docs.text: This paper pairs with the 2025 fibroblast SCF/KIT ILC3 source by showing that SCF/c-Kit is also a lung ILC2 regulatory axis in chronic allergic disease. It helps separate ILC2-SCF and ILC3-SCF branches.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• SCF binds c-Kit, which is expressed by ILC2s in the study context.
  • Antibody targeting SCF248 attenuated chronic asthmatic disease and reduced mast cells, ILC2s, eosinophils, and pathogenic cytokine responses.
  • SCF conditional and c-Kit-defective models supported the importance of SCF/c-Kit activation during chronic allergy.
  • SCF could activate ILC2s in vitro to produce key allergic cytokines.

docs.title: Claim-Level Confidence
docs.text:
• High confidence: SCF/c-Kit signaling regulates ILC2 activation and chronic allergic airway disease severity in this source's mouse models.
  • Medium-high confidence: SCF248 is a candidate disease-modifying axis in chronic allergic disease models.
  • Medium confidence: this source supports a stromal/growth-factor ILC2 regulation branch, but human causality remains inferential.
  • Low confidence: SCF targeting should not be interpreted as a ready clinical asthma therapy from this source alone.

docs.title: Methods and Context
docs.text:
• Chronic allergic airway disease model.
  • SCF248-specific antibody, SCF floxed Col1-Cre-ERT, and W/Wv c-Kit-related evidence.
  • In vitro ILC2 activation assays.
  • Best used for chronic allergic ILC2 regulation and comparison with ILC3 SCF/KIT biology.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• SCF/c-Kit affects multiple cell types including mast cells and eosinophils, so ILC2-specific causality should be worded carefully.
  • Mouse chronic allergy evidence should not be overextended to all human asthma phenotypes.
  • Keep separate from the 2025 fibroblast SCF/KIT ILC3 neutrophilic asthma axis.

docs.title: Contradiction and Supersession
docs.text:
• Contradiction/tension: SCF/KIT can regulate ILC2 in type 2 chronic allergy and ILC3 in neutrophilic asthma; these are related but distinct axes.
  • Supersession status: not superseded; use as the ILC2 SCF/c-Kit chronic allergy branch.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• However, the involvement of innate lymphoid cells (ILCs) in immune responses to infection with Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb) is unknown.
  • Here we show that circulating subsets of ILCs are depleted from the blood of participants with pulmonary tuberculosis and restored upon treatment.
  • Tuberculosis increased accumulation of ILC subsets in the human lung, coinciding with a robust transcriptional response to infection, including a role in orchestrating the recruitment of immune subsets.
  • Using mouse models, we show that group 3 ILCs (ILC3s) accumulated rapidly in Mtb-infected lungs and coincided with the accumulation of alveolar macrophages.
  • Notably, mice that lacked ILC3s exhibited a reduction in the accumulation of early alveolar macrophages and decreased Mtb control.

docs.title: Ingest Mode
docs.text:

  • Meaning: this source page has been manually upgraded for a priority crystallization pass and may support durable topic/entity/project/digest synthesis.

docs.title: Source Type
docs.text:
• primary lung inflammation ILC2 plasticity/pathogenicity study
  • IL-17-producing ST2+ ILC2 source
  • IL-33/leukotriene/AHR signaling and ILC217 pathogenicity paper
  • crystallization status: manually promoted into batch 2 high-priority lung ILC source note

docs.title: Evidence Profile
docs.text:
• Overall confidence: high for IL-33 and leukotriene-driven IL-17 production by ST2+ ILC2s and pathogenic contribution in mouse lung inflammation models.
  • Directness to this project: very high for lung ILC2/ILC3-boundary plasticity and IL-17-linked pathology.
  • Evidence type: papain or IL-33 lung inflammation models, flow cytometry, Rag/Il2rg transfer, Ahr-deficient context, Il17-deficient ILC2 transfer.
  • Main biological axis: papain/IL-33 lung inflammation + leukotriene signals -> Ahr-facilitated ST2+ ILC2 IL-17 production -> more pathogenic lung inflammation.

docs.title: Why It Matters Here
docs.text: This source is a major caution against treating ILC2 as purely type 2. It gives a lung-specific IL-17-producing ST2+ ILC2 branch that intersects with ILC3-like inflammatory interpretation.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Papain or IL-33 treatment boosted IL-17 production from ST2+ ILC2s, termed ILC217s in the source.
  • Ahr facilitated IL-17 production by ILC217s, whereas RORgammat was not the central requirement described in the abstract text.
  • IL-33 and leukotrienes synergized to promote IL-17 production in ILC2s through NFAT-linked signaling.
  • Il17-deficient ILC2s were less pathogenic after transfer in lung inflammation models.

docs.title: Claim-Level Confidence
docs.text:
• High confidence: ST2+ lung ILC2s can produce IL-17 in papain or IL-33-driven lung inflammation models.
  • High confidence: IL-33 and leukotrienes synergize to promote pathogenic IL-17-producing ILC2s in this source.
  • Medium-high confidence: Ahr is an important regulatory node for this ILC2 IL-17 program in the reported model.
  • Low confidence: these cells should not be automatically relabeled as conventional ILC3s without marker and lineage context.

docs.title: Methods and Context
docs.text:
• Mouse papain and IL-33 lung inflammation models.
  • Flow cytometry of lung ILC2 IL-17 production.
  • Ahr-deficient and Il17-deficient transfer contexts.
  • Best used for plastic ILC2/IL-17 pathology, not for canonical ILC3 identity.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• The source's IL-17-producing ILC2s are ST2+ ILC2-like cells, not automatically stable ILC3s.
  • Mouse lung inflammation context should be separated from human asthma claims.
  • IL-17 output coexists with IL-5/IL-13 output in the source, reinforcing mixed effector state interpretation.

docs.title: Contradiction and Supersession
docs.text:
• Contradiction/tension: creates a boundary-state branch between ILC2 and ILC3 inflammatory programs.
  • Supersession status: not superseded; use to qualify simple type 2-only ILC2 statements.

docs.title: Evidence Profile
docs.text:
• Overall confidence: high for human ILC2 plasticity toward IL-17-producing ILCs under IL-1beta, IL-23, and TGF-beta in the source system.
  • Directness to this project: medium-high for airway mucosal plasticity; medium for lung because the main disease tissue is nasal polyp rather than lower lung.
  • Evidence type: human nasal polyp tissue, flow cytometry, in vitro cytokine conversion, epithelial IL-8 induction, association with neutrophilia.

docs.title: Why It Matters Here
docs.text: This source is not a direct lung parenchyma paper, but it is high-value for human airway ILC plasticity. It supports a cautious human correlate for IL-17-producing ILC2-like states seen in mouse lung inflammation.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Human ILC2s transdifferentiated into IL-17-secreting cells in the presence of IL-1beta, IL-23, and TGF-beta.
  • IL-4 or vitamin D3 abrogated this conversion in the reported system.
  • IL-17-producing ILC2-derived cells induced IL-8 secretion by epithelial cells.
  • Their presence in cystic-fibrosis nasal polyps was associated with neutrophilia.

docs.title: Claim-Level Confidence
docs.text:
• High confidence: IL-1beta, IL-23, and TGF-beta can drive human ILC2 plasticity toward IL-17-producing ILCs in this nasal inflammation system.
  • High confidence: IL-4 and vitamin D3 restrain the conversion in the reported assays.
  • Medium confidence: this source is useful for human airway plasticity context relevant to lung ILC interpretation.
  • Low confidence: direct lower-lung disease causality should not be inferred without lung-specific data.

docs.title: Methods and Context
docs.text:
• Human nasal polyp and cystic fibrosis nasal inflammation context.
  • In vitro cytokine conversion with IL-1beta, IL-23, and TGF-beta.
  • Epithelial IL-8 readout and neutrophilia association.
  • Best used as human airway plasticity context, not as direct COPD/asthma lung tissue proof.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• Nasal polyp and cystic fibrosis contexts are not equivalent to lower airway asthma, COPD, or lung infection.
  • Use as human mucosal plasticity evidence with tissue label.
  • Association with neutrophilia should not be overstated as causality in lung disease.

docs.title: Contradiction and Supersession
docs.text:
• Contradiction/tension: supports human IL-17-producing ILC2 plasticity, but tissue context differs from mouse lung ILC217 models.
  • Supersession status: not superseded; use as a human airway plasticity comparator.

docs.title: Source Type
docs.text:
• primary human asthma induced-sputum and macrophage coculture study
  • ILC subset-macrophage polarization source
  • eosinophilic versus noneosinophilic asthma immune-crosstalk paper
  • crystallization status: manually promoted into batch 2 high-priority lung ILC source note

docs.title: Evidence Profile
docs.text:
• Overall confidence: high for association of asthma sputum ILC subsets with macrophage polarization patterns and ex vivo coculture effects in the source.
  • Directness to this project: high for human airway ILC subset disease phenotyping and macrophage crosstalk.
  • Evidence type: induced sputum from steroid-naive asthma subjects and healthy donors, flow cytometry, alveolar macrophage/ILC subset coculture, cytokine and contact-dependent interpretation.
  • Main biological axis: eosinophilic asthma -> ILC2/M2-like macrophage association; noneosinophilic asthma -> ILC1/ILC3/M1-like macrophage association.

docs.title: Why It Matters Here
docs.text: This source connects human airway ILC subset balance to macrophage polarization and asthma phenotype. It complements smoke-associated ILC3/M1 findings and helps interpret ILC2 versus ILC1/3 branches in asthma.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• ILC1s and ILC3s, in addition to ILC2s, were increased in induced sputum from asthma patients relative to healthy donors.
  • ILC2 numbers correlated with M2 macrophages, while ILC1/ILC3 numbers correlated with M1 macrophages.
  • Coculture of ILC2s with alveolar macrophages induced M2-related genes, whereas ILC1/ILC3 coculture induced M1-related genes through cytokine secretion and cell-cell contact.
  • Eosinophilic asthma showed more ILC2/M2 profile, whereas noneosinophilic asthma showed an M1 macrophage-dominant profile.

docs.title: Claim-Level Confidence
docs.text:
• High confidence: airway ILC subset composition differs by asthma phenotype in this human induced-sputum study.
  • High confidence: ILC2s align with M2-like macrophage polarization, while ILC1/ILC3s align with M1-like macrophage polarization in the reported assays.
  • Medium confidence: ILC-macrophage crosstalk may help structure eosinophilic versus noneosinophilic asthma, but patient-level causality remains incompletely proven.
  • Low confidence: induced sputum findings should not be treated as a full map of lung tissue-resident ILC niches.

docs.title: Methods and Context
docs.text:
• Induced sputum from steroid-naive asthma subjects and healthy donors.
  • Flow cytometry of ILC subsets and macrophage phenotypes.
  • Sorted alveolar macrophage and ILC subset coculture.
  • Best used for airway immune-crosstalk and phenotype association.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• Sputum cell composition differs from parenchymal lung tissue.
  • Coculture data support functional capacity but do not fully reconstruct the in vivo airway niche.
  • Steroid-naive cohort context should be kept separate from steroid-resistant asthma claims.

docs.title: Contradiction and Supersession
docs.text:
• Contradiction/tension: ILC2-M2 and ILC1/3-M1 branches should be treated as phenotype-linked patterns, not universal asthma rules.
  • Supersession status: not superseded; use as a human airway crosstalk source.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• n: Fri, 25 Oct 2024 02:50:47 PH81CH19_Barlow ARI 24 December 2018 12:59 Annual Review of Physiology Innate Lymphoid Cells of the Lung Jillian L.
  • All rights reserved Keywords innate lymphoid cell, natural killer cell, lung, asthma Abstract Although, as the major organ of gas exchange, the lung is considered a non- lymphoid organ, an interconnected network of lung-resident innate cells, including epithelial cells, dendritic cells, macrophages, and natural killer cells is crucial for its protection.
  • These cells provide defense against a daily assault by airborne bacteria, viruses, and fungi, as well as prevent the devel- opment of cancer, allergy, and the outgrowth of commensals.
  • Our under- standing of this innate immune environment has recently changed with the discovery of a family of innate lymphoid cells (ILCs): ILC1s, ILC2s, and ILC3s.
  • All lack adaptive antigen receptors but can provide a substantial and rapid source of IFN-γ , IL-5 and IL-13, and IL-17A or IL-22, respectively.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Sonnenberg1,2,3* Interleukin (IL)-2 is a pleiotropic cytokine that is necessary to prevent chronic inflammation in the gastrointestinal tract 1–4.
  • The protective effects of IL-2 involve the generation, maintenance and function of regulatory T (T reg) cells 4–8, and the use of low doses of IL-2 has emerged as a potential therapeutic strategy for patients with inflammatory bowel disease9.
  • However, the cellular and molecular pathways that control the production of IL-2 in the context of intestinal health are undefined.
  • Here we show, in a mouse model, that IL-2 is acutely required to maintain Treg cells and immunological homeostasis throughout the gastrointestinal tract.
  • Notably, lineage-specific deletion of IL-2 in T cells did not reduce Treg cells in the small intestine.

Citation

  • Publication year: 2019
  • DOI: 10.1038/s41423-018-0182-0
  • Metadata source: crossref-doi (confidence: high)
  • Original local title: Kinetics of the accumulation of group 2 innate lymphoid cells in IL-33-induced and IL-25-induced murine models of asthma a potential role for the chemokine CXCL16

docs.title: Ingest Mode
docs.text:
• Mode: focused manual crystallization mode
  • Meaning: this source page has been manually upgraded for a priority crystallization pass and may support durable topic/entity/project/digest synthesis.
  • Required boundary: reusable claims from this source still need source-linked species, tissue, assay, model, and confidence labels.

docs.title: Source Type
docs.text:
• primary research article
  • mouse allergic airway inflammation and ILC2 chemotaxis study
  • IL-33/IL-25/CXCL16-CXCR6 source
  • crystallization status: manually promoted into batch 1 high-priority lung ILC source note

docs.title: Evidence Profile
docs.text:
• Overall confidence: high for IL-33/IL-25-induced lung ILC2 accumulation and CXCL16 direct chemoattractant activity in the reported systems.
  • Directness to this project: high for allergic airway ILC2 recruitment and positioning.
  • Evidence type: flow cytometry, murine asthma-like challenge models, in vitro chemotaxis, small-animal in vivo imaging, pathway activation readouts.
  • Main biological axis: epithelial alarmins IL-33/IL-25 -> lung ILC2 accumulation; CXCL16/CXCR6 -> direct ILC2 migration.

docs.title: Why It Matters Here
docs.text: This source anchors the ILC2 accumulation and trafficking axis in allergic airway models, especially the CXCL16/CXCR6 branch that can be compared against other ILC2 localization and state papers.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• IL-25 and IL-33 induced ILC2 accumulation in mediastinal lymph nodes, lung tissue, and BAL fluid of challenged mice with distinct kinetics and potency.
  • IL-33 and CXCL16 directly induced ILC2 migration in vitro, whereas IL-25 and CCL25 did not in the same assay.
  • Intranasal IL-33 or CXCL16 was sufficient to recruit transferred ILC2s into lungs in vivo.
  • IL-33-induced migration involved ERK1/2, p38, Akt, JNK, and NF-kappaB, while CXCL16-induced migration involved ERK1/2, p38, and Akt.

docs.title: Claim-Level Confidence
docs.text:
• High confidence: IL-33 and IL-25 induce lung ILC2 accumulation in the reported murine asthma-like models.
  • High confidence: CXCL16 has direct chemoattractant activity for ILC2s in the assays used here.
  • Medium confidence: CXCL16/CXCR6 likely contributes to ILC2 positioning in allergic airway inflammation, but source-specific model details should be preserved.
  • Low confidence: this source alone does not establish CXCL16 as the dominant ILC2 trafficking cue in all lung diseases.

docs.title: Methods and Context
docs.text:
• Murine per-nasal OVA, HDM, IL-25, IL-33, CXCL16, and chemokine challenge settings.
  • ILC2 enumeration by flow cytometry across lung tissue, BAL fluid, and mediastinal lymph nodes.
  • In vitro chemotaxis and in vivo transferred-cell imaging.
  • Signaling pathway readouts for chemotaxis-associated activation.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• Mouse asthma-like models; human asthma relevance should be paired with human ILC2 evidence.
  • The paper is strongest for accumulation and migration, not for all downstream effector functions.
  • Automated tags may overstate scRNA-seq or tissue contexts; use the methods above for mature claims.

docs.title: Contradiction and Supersession
docs.text:
• Contradiction/tension: strong CXCL16/CXCR6 trafficking evidence should be reconciled with models where reduced ILC2 abundance does not fully eliminate type 2 cytokine output.
  • Supersession status: not superseded; best used as a direct ILC2 accumulation/chemotaxis source.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Chun et al. unveil a role for the metabolite-sensing receptor Ffar2 (GPR43) in colonic ILC3 populations and delineate how Ffar2 signaling regulates ILC3 function.
  • However, the metabolite-sensing G-protein-coupled receptors that regulate colonic ILC3s remain poorly understood.
  • We found that colonic ILC3s expressed Ffar2, a microbial metabo- lite-sensing receptor, and that Ffar2 agonism pro- moted ILC3 expansion and function.
  • Deﬁciency of Ffar2 in ILC3s decreased their in situ proliferation and ILC3-derived interleukin-22 (IL-22) production.
  • This led to impaired gut epithelial function character- ized by altered mucus-associated proteins and anti- microbial peptides and increased susceptibility to colonic injury and bacterial infection.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Marina Cella, Ramya Gamini. *e-mail: Scott.Jelinsky@pfizer.com; mcolonna@wustl.edu I nnate lymphoid cells (ILCs) are tissue-resident lymphocytes that lack antigen-specific receptors and produce defined cytokines early during the immune response against pathogens 1–3.
  • Three major groups of ILCs are distinguished by the signature cytokines they produce: ILC1s release interferon (IFN)-γ; ILC2s secrete inter - leukin (IL)-5 and IL-13; and ILC3s produce IL-22 and IL-17.
  • Each ILC group responds to distinct stimuli: IL-12, IL-18 and IL-15 trig - ger ILC1s; IL-33, IL-25 and thymic stromal lymphopoietin (TSLP) trigger ILC2s; and IL-23 and IL-1β trigger ILC3s.
  • ILC subtypes are also defined by distinct transcriptional programs and the specific transcription factors that instruct these programs: T-bet and Hobit are critical for ILC1s, high expression of the transcription factor GATA-3 regulates ILC2s, and RORγt and Ahr control ILC3 identity and function 5.
  • The three ILC modules mirror the functional polar - ization of CD4+ T-helper (TH) cells into TH1, TH2 and TH17 cells.

  • Publication year: 2019

  • Original local title: Toll-like receptor 9-dependent interferon production prevents group 2 innate lymphoid cell-driven airway hyperreactivity

docs.title: Ingest Mode
docs.text:

  • Meaning: this source page was read beyond bulk registration and now records source-specific biological claims, model context, assay directness, and reuse boundaries.
  • Durable synthesis status: selected high-confidence claims from this source were propagated into ILC2 entity/topic/digest pages with source links.

docs.title: Source Type
docs.text:
• primary mouse and humanized-mouse allergic-airway ILC2 regulation study
  • Evidence profile: CpG A and MIS416 TLR9 activation, IL-33-induced AHR, Alternaria allergic asthma model, NK/type I IFN/IFN-gamma axis, STAT1-dependent ILC2 suppression, and humanized NSG ILC2 assays.
  • Knowledge note status: focused source note suitable for wiki synthesis, with caveats preserved below.

docs.title: Evidence Profile
docs.text:
• Overall confidence: high for source-specific claims explicitly tied to the reported model, species, assay, and outcome; lower for cross-disease or human translation unless directly tested.
  • Evidence tags: #cell/ILC2 #tissue/lung #topic/regulation #topic/pulmonary_disease #axis/ILC2_niche #axis/interferon_brake #axis/type2_immunity #status/focused_crystallization
  • Primary biological axis: This paper links microbial/TLR9 sensing to suppression of ILC2-driven airway hyperreactivity through an interferon cascade, giving the wiki a disease-relevant inhibitory branch.

docs.title: Why It Matters Here
docs.text: This paper links microbial/TLR9 sensing to suppression of ILC2-driven airway hyperreactivity through an interferon cascade, giving the wiki a disease-relevant inhibitory branch.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• TLR9 activation suppresses IL-33-mediated airway hyperreactivity and airway inflammation through inhibition of ILC2s.
  • The suppressive pathway involves type I interferon-driven NK-cell IFN-gamma production and direct IFN-gamma/STAT1 signaling in ILC2s.
  • MIS416 suppresses Alternaria-induced allergic airway inflammation and inhibits human ILC2 expansion/function in humanized NSG or ex vivo-linked systems described by the source.
  • This paper supports IFN-gamma as a therapeutically interesting but context-dependent brake on ILC2-driven type 2 airway disease.

docs.title: Claim-Level Confidence
docs.text:
• High confidence: TLR9 activation can inhibit ILC2-driven AHR in the reported mouse allergic-airway systems.
  • High confidence: IFN-gamma and STAT1 are source-supported direct inhibitory nodes for ILC2 function.
  • Medium confidence: MIS416 and TLR9 agonism remain preclinical/translational hypotheses, not clinical recommendations for asthma.

docs.title: Methods and Context
docs.text:
• Species/context: mouse IL-33 and Alternaria airway inflammation plus humanized/ex vivo ILC2 evidence.
  • Assay directness: strong perturbation evidence for TLR9/interferon/STAT1-mediated ILC2 inhibition.
  • Best wiki use: microbial-sensing brake, IFN-gamma/STAT1 ILC2 suppression, allergic AHR regulation.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• Separate TLR9 agonist effects on the whole immune system from ILC2-intrinsic IFN-gamma/STAT1 signaling.
  • Do not present MIS416 as established asthma therapy.

docs.title: Contradiction and Supersession
docs.text:
• Contradiction status: this source should be integrated as a context-specific branch, not as a universal statement about ILC2 biology.
  • Supersession status: this source refines earlier ILC2 models by adding spatial, interferon, neuroimmune, developmental, or type 2 framework detail; it does not supersede lung repair, allergic inflammation, or ILC2 plasticity branches.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Elevated numbers of basophils in the periphery is a highly con - served characteristic of type 2 cytokine responses 10,11.
  • Although basophils are known to secrete robust amounts of IL-4 upon activation, basophils can also produce additional soluble media - tors, including cytokines (IL-6, tumor necrosis factor and IL-13), inflammatory molecules (histamine and platelet-activating fac - tor), bioactive lipids (prostaglandins and leukotrienes), as well as growth factors (amphiregulin and macrophage colony-stimulating factor 1) 10,11.
  • Given their capacity to secrete these potent effector molecules, it is not surprising that basophils are reported to oper - ate as important mediators of allergic inflammation 12–14.
  • For example, studies have demonstrated that basophils imprint on alveolar macrophages and play important roles during lung development, suggesting that the functions of basophils are context-dependent and extend beyond their ability to promote inflammation 15,16.
  • While studies have dem - onstrated that basophils promote type 2 inflammation and worm expulsion following Trichuris muris and Trichinella spiralis infec - tions, their functions following other helminth infections remain to be fully defined 17–19.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Nature | Vol 579 | 26 March 2020 | 575 Article Feeding-dependent VIP neuron–ILC3 circuit regulates the intestinal barrier Jhimmy Talbot1, Paul Hahn1, Lina Kroehling1, Henry Nguyen1, Dayi Li1 & Dan R.
  • Here we show in mice how a gut neuronal signal triggered by food intake is integrated with intestinal antimicrobial and metabolic responses that are controlled by type-3 innate lymphoid cells (ILC3) 1– 3.
  • Food consumption rapidly activates a population of enteric neurons that express vasoactive intestinal peptide (VIP) 4.
  • Projections of VIP-producing neurons (VIPergic neurons) in the lamina propria are in close proximity to clusters of ILC3 that selectively express VIP receptor type 2 (VIPR2; also known as VPAC2).
  • Production of interleukin (IL)-22 by ILC3, which is upregulated by the presence of commensal microorganisms such as segmented filamentous bacteria 5– 7, is inhibited upon engagement of VIPR2.

  • Publication year: 2020

  • Metadata source: crossref-doi (confidence: high)
  • Original local title: IL-1beta prevents ILC2 expansion, type 2 cytokine secretion, and mucus metaplasia in response to early-life rhinovirus infection in mice

docs.title: Ingest Mode
docs.text:
• Mode: focused manual crystallization mode
  • Meaning: this source page has been manually upgraded for a priority crystallization pass and may support durable topic/entity/project/digest synthesis.
  • Required boundary: reusable claims from this source still need source-linked species, tissue, assay, model, and confidence labels.

docs.title: Source Type
docs.text:
• primary research article
  • early-life rhinovirus mouse model
  • IL-1beta/NLRP3 negative regulation of ILC2/type 2 pathology source
  • crystallization status: manually promoted into batch 1 high-priority lung ILC source note

docs.title: Evidence Profile
docs.text:
• Overall confidence: high for IL-1beta restraining ILC2 expansion, type 2 cytokines, mucus metaplasia, and AHR in the reported early-life rhinovirus model.
  • Directness to this project: high for virus-conditioned regulation of lung ILC2 function.
  • Evidence type: neonatal/early-life mouse rhinovirus infection, IL-1 receptor antagonist, anti-IL-1beta, NLRP3 knockout, recombinant IL-1beta intervention.
  • Main biological axis: early-life rhinovirus -> IL-25/IL-33/type 2 program plus NLRP3/IL-1beta brake -> restrained ILC2 and mucus/AHR outcomes.

docs.title: Why It Matters Here
docs.text: This source is important because it shows that not every inflammatory cytokine amplifies ILC2 disease. In early-life rhinovirus-like disease, IL-1beta acts as a brake on ILC2 expansion and type 2 airway pathology.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Rhinovirus infection in immature mice induced IL-25, IL-33, type 2 cytokine transcripts, ILC2 expansion, mucus metaplasia, and airway hyperresponsiveness.
  • Rhinovirus also induced NLRP3 inflammasome-associated IL-1beta production, with lung macrophages described as a major source.
  • Blocking IL-1beta signaling or removing NLRP3 enhanced ILC2/type 2/mucus outcomes in the source text.
  • Recombinant IL-1beta logic supports IL-1beta as a negative regulator of this ILC2-driven asthma-like phenotype.

docs.title: Claim-Level Confidence
docs.text:
• High confidence: IL-1beta restrains ILC2 expansion and type 2 airway pathology in this early-life rhinovirus mouse model.
  • High confidence: the source places NLRP3/IL-1beta signaling as a counter-regulatory branch during virus-induced ILC2 activation.
  • Medium confidence: the macrophage-derived IL-1beta brake is relevant to lung immune crosstalk, but cell-source details should be checked in full figures for manuscript use.
  • Low confidence: this source should not be generalized to adult rhinovirus infection or all IL-1beta/ILC2 contexts without additional evidence.

docs.title: Methods and Context
docs.text:
• Six-day-old mouse rhinovirus RV-A1B infection model.
  • Wild-type and NLRP3-deficient mice.
  • IL-1RA, anti-IL-1beta, and recombinant IL-1beta interventions.
  • Readouts include ILC2 expansion, type 2 cytokines, mucin-related transcripts, mucus metaplasia, and AHR.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• Early-life mouse model; adult lung and human pediatric asthma require separate evidence.
  • IL-1beta can have different effects in other ILC contexts, including ILC3 activation or ILC2 plasticity.
  • This source is a negative-regulation branch, not evidence that IL-1beta is broadly anti-inflammatory.

docs.title: Contradiction and Supersession
docs.text:
• Contradiction/tension: IL-1beta restrains ILC2 here, while IL-1beta can activate ILC3/neutrophilic programs in other asthma contexts.
  • Supersession status: not superseded; keep as early-life rhinovirus ILC2 brake source.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• BRIEF DEFINITIVE REPORT ILC2s are the predominant source of intestinal ILC-derived IL-10 Jennifer K.
  • Here, we report the absence of an intestinal regulatory ILC population distinct from group 1 ILCs (ILC1s), ILC2s, and ILC3s in (1) mice bred in our animal facility; (2) mice from The Jackson Laboratory, Taconic Biosciences, and Charles River Laboratories; and (3) mice subjected to intestinal inflammation.
  • Instead, a low percentage of intestinal ILC2s produced IL-10 at steady state.
  • A screen for putative IL-10 elicitors revealed that IL-2, IL-4, IL-27, IL-10, and neuromedin U (NMU) increased IL-10 production in activated intestinal ILC2s, while TL1A suppressed IL-10 production.
  • Secreted IL-10 further induced IL-10 production in ILC2s through a positive feedback loop.

docs.title: Source Type
docs.text:
• primary research article
  • neonatal lung ILC3 developmental niche study
  • mouse lung development with human premature infant/BPD relevance
  • crystallization status: manually promoted into batch 1 high-priority lung ILC source note

docs.title: Evidence Profile
docs.text:
• Overall confidence: high for alveolar fibroblast-derived IGF1 supporting postnatal pulmonary ILC3 development in the reported mouse system.
  • Directness to this project: high for developmental lung ILC3 niche biology; medium for adult inflammatory lung disease.
  • Evidence type: developmental lung niche mapping, conditional IGF1/IGF1R perturbation, pneumonia susceptibility, human premature infant/BPD comparison.
  • Main biological axis: alveolar fibroblast IGF1 -> ILC precursor expansion/maturation -> pulmonary ILC3 development -> neonatal antibacterial defense.

docs.title: Why It Matters Here
docs.text: This source anchors the tissue-niche branch of lung ILC3 biology. It shows that pulmonary ILC3 abundance and function are shaped by developmental stromal signals, not only by inflammatory cytokines during disease.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Pulmonary ILC3s descended from ILC precursors occupying a spatially distinct developing-lung niche.
  • Alveolar fibroblast-derived IGF1 instructed postnatal expansion and maturation of pulmonary ILC precursors.
  • Conditional loss of fibroblast IGF1 or ILC precursor IGF1 receptor disrupted pulmonary ILC3 biogenesis and increased newborn mouse susceptibility to pneumonia.
  • Premature infants with bronchopulmonary dysplasia showed reduced IGF1 and pulmonary ILC3 numbers in the source text.

docs.title: Claim-Level Confidence
docs.text:
• High confidence: alveolar fibroblast-derived IGF1 supports neonatal pulmonary ILC3 development in this source.
  • High confidence: the source links lung developmental programs with mucosal immune defense against pneumonia.
  • Medium confidence: human premature infant/BPD observations support translational relevance but do not by themselves establish causality in humans.
  • Low confidence: adult asthma or ARDS claims should not be inferred from this source without additional adult disease evidence.

docs.title: Methods and Context
docs.text:
• Developmental mouse lung niche analysis.
  • Conditional manipulation of IGF1 or IGF1 receptor pathways.
  • Pneumonia susceptibility as functional readout.
  • Human premature infant/BPD comparison included as translational context.
  • Note: the local filename year appears inconsistent with the article text, which identifies a 2020 Immunity paper.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• Strongest causal evidence is in neonatal mouse lung.
  • Human data should be treated as supportive association unless manually reviewed in full detail.
  • Developmental niche biology should not be merged automatically with adult inflammatory fibroblast-ILC3 axes.

docs.title: Contradiction and Supersession
docs.text:
• Contradiction/tension: developmental IGF1 niche support differs from inflammatory SCF/KIT fibroblast-ILC3 activation in neutrophilic asthma.
  • Supersession status: not superseded; best treated as the neonatal developmental niche branch.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Karagiannis and colleagues reveal that external lipids transiently stored in lipid droplets are required to fuel pathogenic ILC2 responses and identify a ketogenic diet as a potent intervention strategy to treat ILC2-driven airway inﬂammation.

  • Here, we show that tis- sue-resident type 2 ILCs (ILC2s) display a distinct metabolic signature upon chronic activation.
  • In the context of allergen-driven airway inﬂammation, ILC2s increase their uptake of both external lipids and glucose.
  • Externally acquired fatty acids are tran- siently stored in lipid droplets and converted into phospholipids to promote the proliferation of ILC2s.

docs.text:
• primary ILC2 checkpoint/metabolism airway inflammation study
  • crystallization status: manually promoted into focused manual crystallization batch 3
  • Batch 3 axis: PD-1 metabolic checkpoint on ILC2

docs.title: Evidence Profile
docs.text:
• Overall confidence: medium-high to high for the source-specific claims listed below; lower for broad extrapolation beyond the reported system.
  • Evidence profile: IL-33-induced pulmonary ILC2 activation, PD-1 deficiency, metabolic profiling, humanized mouse PD-1 agonist intervention.
  • Batch 3 synthesis role: PD-1 metabolic checkpoint on ILC2.

docs.title: Why It Matters Here
docs.text: This source makes PD-1 a metabolic brake on ILC2 activation, creating a checkpoint branch distinct from PD-1 blockade in cancer.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• PD-1 limits ILC2 viability and downregulates effector function during IL-33-induced airway inflammation.
  • PD-1 deficiency shifts ILC2 metabolism toward glycolysis, glutaminolysis, and methionine catabolism.
  • PD-1 blockade worsens AHR in the reported system, while a PD-1 agonist ameliorates AHR and lung inflammation in a humanized mouse model.

docs.title: Claim-Level Confidence
docs.text:
• High confidence: PD-1 acts as a metabolic checkpoint limiting pulmonary ILC2 activation in the reported models.
  • Medium-high confidence: PD-1 agonism is a mechanistically coherent preclinical intervention, but not a clinical recommendation.
  • Low confidence: do not assume PD-1 pathway manipulation has the same effect across cancer and asthma contexts.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• In 1986, a landmark publication by Tim Mossman and Robert Coffman described two subsets of CD4 + T cells, known as T helper 1 (T H1) cells and T H2 cells, that differed in their cytokine production profiles.
  • TH1 cells produce interferon- γ (IFNγ), whereas T H2 cells produce IL-4 and IL-5 (ref.
  • In 2005, a third subset of CD4+ T cells that produce IL-17 was described (known as TH17 cells)2,3.
  • In the following years, two further CD4+ T cell populations that produce IL-22 or IL-9 as signa- ture cytokines were described (TH22 cells and TH9 cells, respectively)4–6.
  • This subdivision based on cytokine pro- duction profiles turned out not to be unique to CD4 + T cells expressing the αβ T cell receptor (TCR) as it was also observed for natural killer T (NKT) cells and T cells expressing the γδ TCR7.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Innate lymphoid cells (ILCs) are predominantly tissue-resident, and accumulating data indicate that they have signi ﬁcant tissue-speciﬁc functions.
  • We summarize current knowledge of ILC phenotypes in various tissues in mice and humans, aiming to clarify ILC immunity in distinct anatomical locations.
  • Fundamental Characteristics of Innate Lymphoid Cells Innate lymphoid cells (ILCs) comprise non-T, non-B, lymphoid cells in mice and humans.
  • A univer- sal nomenclature proposed in 2018 [ 1] segregated ILCs into ﬁve distinct subsets: natural killer (NK) cells, ILC1, ILC2, ILC3, and lymphoid tissue inducer cells (LTi, see Glossary), of which only NK cells and ILC1, the latter in some tissues, demonstrate cytotoxic activity [ 2].
  • Although cy- totoxic CD56 dim NK cells lack the interleukin (IL)-7 receptor subunit α (IL-7R-α, also known as CD127), and CD56 bright NK cells express intermediate amounts, ILCs show high expression of CD127 [3,4].

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Nature | Vol 600 | 23/30 December 2021 | 707 Article Antigen-presenting innate lymphoid cells orchestrate neuroinflammation John B.
  • Here we define a population of inflammatory group 3 innate lymphoid cells (ILC3s) that infiltrate the CNS in a mouse model of multiple sclerosis.
  • These ILC3s are derived from the circulation, localize in proximity to infiltrating T cells in the CNS, function as antigen-presenting cells that restimulate myelin-specific T cells, and are increased in individuals with multiple sclerosis.
  • Notably, antigen presentation by inflammatory ILC3s is required to promote T cell responses in the CNS and the development of multiple-sclerosis-like disease in mouse models.
  • By contrast, conventional and tissue-resident ILC3s in the periphery do not appear to contribute to disease induction, but instead limit autoimmune T cell responses and prevent multiple-sclerosis-like disease when experimentally targeted to present myelin antigen.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• In mice, ILC3s regulate adaptive immunity, shape the microbiota composition, and protect from tumor progression as well as colorectal cancer immunotherapy resistance.
  • Here, we identify that colorectal cancer (CRC) manifests with altered ILC3s that are characterized by reduced frequencies, increased plasticity, and an imbalance with T cells.
  • We evaluated the consequences of these changes in mice and determined that a dialog between ILC3s and T cells via major histocompatibility complex class II (MHCII) is necessary to support colonization with microbiota that subsequently induce type- 1 immunity in the intestine and tumor microenvironment.
  • As a result, mice lacking ILC3-speciﬁc MHCII develop invasive CRC and resistance to anti-PD-1 immunotherapy.
  • Finally, humans with dysregulated intes- tinal ILC3s harbor microbiota that fail to induce type-1 immunity and immunotherapy responsiveness when transferred to mice.

docs.title: Source Type
docs.text:
• primary research letter/article
  • papain/protease-driven allergic lung pathology model
  • ILC3-derived acetylcholine and IL-17-associated pathology source
  • crystallization status: manually promoted into batch 1 high-priority lung ILC source note

docs.title: Evidence Profile
docs.text:
• Overall confidence: medium-high for ILC3 involvement in papain-driven allergic lung pathology and acetylcholine association in this model.
  • Directness to this project: high for noncanonical ILC3 mediator function in allergic lung pathology.
  • Evidence type: acute papain challenge, RAG2-deficient and Rorc-deficient contexts, cytokine and BAL inflammation readouts, acetylcholine synthesis framing.
  • Main biological axis: protease allergen/papain -> IL-23/IL-17-associated ILC3 response -> ILC3-derived acetylcholine -> allergic lung pathology.

docs.title: Why It Matters Here
docs.text: This source broadens ILC3 lung disease biology beyond IL-22 and IL-17 alone by adding a noncanonical acetylcholine-producing ILC3 branch in protease-driven allergic inflammation.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Papain challenge increased lung cytokines including IL-13, IL-17A, IL-22, and IL-23.
  • IL-23 neutralization in RAG2-deficient mice reduced papain-induced inflammatory features and IL-17A while preserving the idea that non-adaptive lymphoid cells contribute.
  • Rorc-deficient mice showed reduced ILC3-associated responses and reduced detection of IL-13, IL-17A, and IL-22 in the reported context.
  • The study identified ILC3-derived acetylcholine synthesis as associated with protease-driven lung pathology.

docs.title: Claim-Level Confidence
docs.text:
• High confidence: papain/protease-driven allergic lung pathology engages an IL-23/IL-17-associated innate lymphoid axis in this source.
  • Medium-high confidence: ILC3-derived acetylcholine is a source-supported noncanonical mediator branch in this model.
  • Medium confidence: this source is relevant to allergic lung pathology but should be interpreted as acute protease-driven disease, not all asthma.
  • Low confidence: extension to chronic human asthma or therapeutic cholinergic targeting requires additional evidence.

docs.title: Methods and Context
docs.text:
• Acute papain/protease allergic lung challenge.
  • RAG2-deficient and Rorc-deficient model logic used to separate adaptive and RORgammat-associated contributions.
  • Cytokine and BAL inflammatory readouts.
  • Focus on ILC3-derived acetylcholine as noncanonical mediator.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• This is a protease/papain model and should not be generalized to all allergic airway disease.
  • The paper format/source extraction begins near references; focused manual PDF inspection is advised before manuscript-level use.
  • RORgammat deficiency can affect multiple cell types and developmental programs.

docs.title: Contradiction and Supersession
docs.text:
• Contradiction/tension: ILC3s can contribute to allergic pathology here, while other ILC3 sources emphasize bacterial defense or developmental protection.
  • Supersession status: not superseded; keep as the acetylcholine/protease-allergy ILC3 branch.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• In this study, Dong et al. demonstrate that intestine epithelial cells-derived IL-17D serve as a critical factor in regulating ILC3s function and intestinal homeostasis by binding the receptor CD93.
  • Here, we examined the role of IL-17D, a poorly understood member in the IL-17 family.
  • Il17d /C0//C0mice were more susceptible to acute colitis, bacterial infection and experimentally induced colon cancer than their wild- type counterparts.
  • Il17d deﬁciency impaired IL-22 production by group 3 innate lymphoid cells (ILC3s) and reduced expression of IL-22-dependent antimicrobial peptides, RegIIIb and RegIIIg, in colon tissue at steady state and in colitis; this was associated with changes in microbial composition and dysbiosis.
  • Protein puri- ﬁcation studies revealed that IL-17D bound not canonical IL-17 receptors, but rather CD93, a glycoprotein expressed on mature ILC3s.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• ted: 14 February 2021 DOI: 10.1111/all.14836 ORIGINAL ARTICLE Asthma and Lower Airway Disease Long- acting muscarinic antagonist regulates group 2 innate lymphoid cell- dependent airway eosinophilic inflammation Takahiro Matsuyama1 | Kentaro Machida1 | Yasutaka Motomura2,3 | Koichi Takagi1 | Yoichi Doutake1 | Asako Tanoue- Hamu1 | Kiyotaka Kondo1 | Keiko Mizuno1 | Kazuyo Moro2,3,4 | Hiromasa Inoue1 © 2021 EAACI and John Wiley and Sons A/S.
  • Abbreviations: ACh, Acetylcholine; BALF, Bronchoalveolar lavage fluids; COPD, Chronic pulmonary obstructive disease; CysLT, Cysteinyl leukotriene; ICS, Inhaled corticosteroid; ILC2, Group 2 innate lymphoid cell; LABA, Long- acting β 2 agonist; LAMA, Long- acting muscarinic antagonist; Lin, Lineage; M3R, Muscarinic M3 receptor; OVA, Ovalbumin; PBMC, Peripheral blood mononuclear cell; PCR, Polymerase chain reaction; Th2 cell, T helper type 2 cell; TSLP, Thymic stromal lymphopoietin.
  • Email: inoue@m2.kufm.kagoshima-u.ac.jp Funding information Japan Society for the Promotion of Science, Grant/Award Number: 17K09661; AMED, Grant/Award Number: 20ek0410052h0003; Boehringer Ingelheim Abstract Background: Tiotropium bromide, a long- acting muscarinic antagonist, reduces the frequency of exacerbation in patients with moderate to severe asthma, but its un - derlying mechanism is not clear.
  • Asthma exacerbations are associated with exposure to external stimuli, and group 2 innate lymphoid cells (ILC2s) are considered to be involved in the pathophysiology of asthma exacerbation.
  • We investigated whether tiotropium modulates airway inflammation through ILC2 functions.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Patients with severe asthma have unmet clinical needs for effective and safe therapies.
  • One possibility may be mesenchymal stem cell (MSC) therapy, which can improve asthma in murine models.
  • However, it remains unclear how MSCs exert their beneficial effects in asthma.
  • Here, we examined the effect of human umbilical cord blood-derived MSCs (hUC-MSC) on two mouse models of severe asthma, namely, Alternaria alternata-induced and house dust mite (HDM)/diesel exhaust particle (DEP)-induced asthma. hUC- MSC treatment attenuated lung type 2 (Th2 and type 2 innate lymphoid cell) inflammation in both models.
  • In vitro co-culture showed that hUC- MSC directly downregulated the interleukin (IL)-5 and IL-13 production of differentiated mouse Th2 cells and peripheral blood mononuclear cells from asthma patients.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• l: matthew.hepworth@manchester.ac.uk; d.withers@bham.ac.uk I LCs are integral to the maintenance of tissue homeostasis1, func- tioning to interpret a wealth of environmental cues and translate these signals into appropriate responses2.
  • This role is orchestrated through production of effector cytokine profiles once considered limited to CD4 + helper T (T H) cell subsets 3.
  • Group 1 ILCs (ILC1s) express interferon (IFN)-γ and T-bet, group 2 ILCs (ILC2s) produce interleukin (IL)-4, IL-5, IL-9 and IL-13 and express high levels of GATA3, while group 3 ILCs (ILC3s) are characterized by produc - tion of IL-22 and IL-17 and RORγt expression 4.
  • However, unlike T cells, ILCs do not require the provision of polarizing signals or antigen to express subset-associated TFs during development, nor to acquire core effector functions5.
  • Nonetheless, ILCs exhibit exten- sive plasticity and heterogeneity, even within subgroups that express the same ‘master’ TFs6,7.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Specific lineage commitment is dependent on the interplay of discrete stromal microenvironments, providing intercellular signals via cell contact and cytokines and the induction of lineage-restricted transcription factors (TFs).
  • Although the thymus provides a specialized environment that promotes T cell differentiation, T cell progenitors in the adult thymus retain ILC2 potential until the DN2 stage7 and also produce natural killer (NK) cells8.
  • The presence of ILC2-like cells was noted in adult and embryonic thymi 9,10, but the development of ILC2 cells from ETPs in vivo remains to be demonstrated, as do the critical factors that distinguish T cell and ILC2 fates.
  • Both T cell and ILC2 differ - entiation are co-dependent on Notch and interleukin (IL)-7 recep - tor (IL-7R) signaling and share TFs including GATA-3, BCL11B and TCF-1 (refs.
  • T cell differentiation is favored by low concentrations of IL-7, while ILC2 development requires higher IL-7 concentrations 13.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Flavell1,9 ✉ Tissue-resident innate lymphoid cells (ILCs) help sustain barrier function and respond to local signals.
  • ILCs are traditionally classified as ILC1, ILC2 or ILC3 on the basis of their expression of specific transcription factors and cytokines1.
  • In the skin, disease- specific production of ILC3-associated cytokines interleukin (IL)-17 and IL-22 in response to IL-23 signalling contributes to dermal inflammation in psoriasis.
  • However, it is not known whether this response is initiated by pre-committed ILCs or by cell-state transitions.
  • Here we show that the induction of psoriasis in mice by IL-23 or imiquimod reconfigures a spectrum of skin ILCs, which converge on a pathogenic ILC3-like state.

  • Main biological axis: IL-33/ST2 feed-forward support -> BATF-dependent wound-healing ILC2 program -> lung tissue protection; BATF loss -> ILC3-like pathogenic shift.

docs.title: Why It Matters Here
docs.text: This source is a high-value bridge between ILC2 repair and ILC plasticity. It explains why lung ILC2 identity maintenance matters during acute viral infection.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• A transcriptionally distinct lung ILC2 subset enriched for wound-healing genes and BATF was identified during acute respiratory viral infection.
  • BATF promoted ILC2 proliferation and function and restricted ILC2 plasticity during influenza infection.
  • Loss of BATF caused ILC2s to lose protective properties and acquire pathogenic ILC3-like functions.
  • BATF directly bound regulatory elements of wound-healing genes, maintained chromatin accessibility, and participated in an IL-33/ST2 feed-forward loop.

docs.title: Claim-Level Confidence
docs.text:
• High confidence: BATF supports protective, wound-healing-enriched ILC2 identity during acute respiratory viral infection.
  • High confidence: BATF loss promotes harmful ILC2 plasticity toward ILC3-like inflammatory functions in this model.
  • Medium confidence: BATF is a candidate therapeutic target for inflammatory resolution, but intervention strategy and human translation need separate support.
  • Low confidence: this source should not be used to claim all ILC2-to-ILC3-like plasticity is BATF-dependent.

docs.title: Methods and Context
docs.text:
• Acute influenza virus infection model.
  • Single-cell RNA-seq and regulatory/chromatin analyses.
  • BATF perturbation used to test identity, proliferation, and function.
  • Readouts include neutrophil infiltration, tissue damage, respiratory failure, and wound-healing gene expression.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• Strongest evidence is in acute viral infection, not chronic asthma.
  • BATF effects may depend on infection timing and tissue state.
  • Human translation remains to be validated.

docs.title: Contradiction and Supersession
docs.text:
• Contradiction/tension: integrates repair and pathology by showing that ILC2 identity maintenance can prevent ILC3-like harmful functions.
  • Supersession status: not superseded; it refines earlier influenza ILC repair/pathology papers mechanistically.

Citation
